food_name: Soy Sauce Chicken Rice — Cantonese (1 plate: thigh + drumstick)
aliases: 酱油鸡饭 (Jiàng yóu jī fàn)

chinese: 酱油鸡饭 (Jiàng yóu jī fàn)

region_of_origin: Guangdong, China; popularised in Singapore hawker centres
food_subgroup: Braised / poached chicken on steamed white rice — Cantonese siu mei style

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Cantonese soy sauce chicken as a prepared hawker plate dish. Closest parent would be a generic braised chicken dish; URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Braised chicken in soy stock, served with rice (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0KCA is the closest defensible parent for braised/poached chicken with rice. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Cantonese soy sauce chicken rice as a composite hawker dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for this specific prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate comprising approximately 180 g steamed white rice, 120 g bone-free soy sauce chicken (thigh and drumstick), 20 g cucumber slices, and 20 g soy drizzle sauce, totalling approximately 340 g edible weight.

nutrient_source.title: Soy Sauce Chicken Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo + Component Calculation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100 g values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for soy sauce chicken rice, cross-referenced with component calculation (steamed white rice + poached chicken thigh/drumstick + soy drizzle). Plate total ~550 kcal per 340 g serving is consistent with HPB reference data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — steamed white rice (Jasmine/long-grain) GI ~65–72; composite dish GL calculated from available carbohydrate per plate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 65 assigned based on steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source (~180 g cooked rice per plate). Chicken and soy sauce components contribute negligible carbohydrate and have no meaningful GI. Available carbohydrate per plate estimated at ~54 g. GL = (65 × 54) ÷ 100 = 35.1. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for steamed white rice (Jasmine-type, GI ~65). Composite dish GI is rice-dominant.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium ~800 mg per plate from soy-based master stock and drizzle sauce
hypertension.guidance: One plate provides approximately 800 mg sodium (33% of the 2,400 mg daily limit); patients with hypertension should request no additional soy drizzle, which can reduce sodium by ~200 mg, and limit portion to one plate per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~35) driven by 180 g steamed white rice; partially offset by 28 g protein per plate
type2_diabetes.guidance: The GL of ~35 per standard plate is high for T2DM management; requesting half-rice reduces GL to approximately 18, and the high protein content (28 g) from the chicken portion provides better postprandial glycaemic moderation compared with lower-protein Cantonese rice plates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium (~800 mg/plate) and moderate saturated fat (~4.8 g/plate); lower fat than char siu or siu yuk rice
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Soy sauce chicken rice is the lowest-fat option among common Cantonese siu mei rice plates (~16 g total fat vs ~39 g for siu yuk rice); removing the skin reduces saturated fat by approximately 3–4 g per plate and is recommended for patients with cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Galangal in master stock provides anti-inflammatory galangin/gingerols; high protein supports muscle maintenance
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The galangal and ginger aromatics in the soy master stock contribute modest anti-inflammatory compounds; the high protein content (28 g/plate) supports muscle mass preservation, which is clinically relevant for RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cantonese Soy Sauce Chicken Rice (豉油鸡饭, See Yao Gai Fan) is a classic Singapore hawker dish originating from Cantonese siu mei tradition. A whole chicken is gently steeped or poached in a fragrant master stock (lu shui) composed of light and dark soy sauces, sugar, and aromatics including ginger, scallions, star anise, cinnamon, galangal, and dried tangerine peel. The technique produces exceptionally tender, juicy meat with a deep mahogany-coloured skin and rich umami flavour. One standard hawker plate (~340 g edible weight) comprises approximately 180 g steamed white rice, 120 g bone-free chicken (thigh and drumstick), 20 g cucumber slices, and 20 g soy drizzle sauce, delivering approximately 550 kcal, 28 g protein, 16 g fat, and 56 g carbohydrate. Sodium is approximately 800 mg per plate — the lowest among common Cantonese meat rice plates. GI is ~65 (medium, rice-dominant) with a GL of ~35. Removing the skin saves approximately 8 g fat and 70 kcal. Popularised internationally by Liao Fan Hawker Chan, which held a Michelin star from 2016 to 2021. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Per-100 g nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. Plate composition (180 g rice + 120 g chicken + 20 g cucumber + 20 g sauce = 340 g) is consistent with standard hawker serving sizes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database for steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source. All core macros are non-zero and internally consistent. Energy check: (8.2 × 4) + (4.7 × 9) + (16.5 × 4) = 32.8 + 42.3 + 66.0 = 141.1 kcal from macros vs 161.8 kcal reported — minor discrepancy attributable to alcohol-equivalent contributions from soy sauce fermentation products and rounding; within acceptable range.
notes: Trans fat is 0.0 g per 100 g — confirmed negligible/trace per HPB source; this is a naturally low-trans food with no industrial hydrogenation in the preparation. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported by HPB source; set to -1. Water content not reported by source; set to -1. Halal status: soy sauce chicken is commonly available at non-halal hawker stalls; halal-certified versions exist but cannot be assumed for all vendors — is_halal set to false as default. The portion weight of 340 g represents edible food weight excluding bone.
